item.title: It's time for yet another unhinged XL Saturday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
way to be a neutral commentator
as is tradition
radio shack had em (jk)
teeth in the stomach.. brilliant
bc building dams is awesome?
slaps fairly hard
mmm hmm
would love to meet some fellow dumpers. i steal so much from them i feel like i know them
kinda need to see Silence of the Lambs for this one
i guess
please don't get your pets high gang
yes
not 'ha ha' funny
wondering who the bird man that children should see is
what's your favorite dinosaur, gang?
love me a good courtesy phone
that's my set.. have a great night gang!!

item.dc:creator: TearEmUpTara

item.title: Our Pantry Makeover: See the Before and after
item.description:
Finished
Before
Made a mock up in Fusion 360, exported to iPhone and used the native AR file viewer to place the model into the pantry to see what it could look like
The old out
Concrete was very uneven. We wanted all walls to look similar with mudding
First mudding done
Splitting wood to make supports for the shelves
Supports are up
The shelving could not fit i to the room as one piece. So I decided to split then up. Made a quick jig to draw out a path which I just cut with a jigsaw
Placing the first shelves
Glued and shot a nail. Used the clamp to avid the wood splitting. Added acrylic and mud after the glue was dry.
Time to add acrylic all around and paint
First coat with paint gun. Sanding needed and applied a second coat
Added Philips Hue light strips around the door, and set up a motion sensor to trigger it and the ceiling light
Testing motion sensor
It's fun to compare this video to the first simulation I did. Surprised over the similarities
item.dc:creator: DIYguy

Speaking of miracles…
Hail Eris!
This is where I post from
Sleepy cat tax
Ok space lady I love you bye bye!
item.dc:creator: mothmam

item.title: Nobody else to say this to
item.description: I've known her for 35 years. We've been friends, a couple, a married couple, a divorced couple, FWB. She lived with me while fighting breast cancer. We have four kids and have remained very close during the 17+ years after our divorce. My kids are all in their 20s, but I worry for how this is impacting and will impact them. But I'm handling it badly. When I or my ex have had major issues we always end up talking to each other about them. She's been my sanity check and my reality anchor during some tough times. I love her as much as when I first met her, even if we didn't work as a couple. I'm sitting out of state trying to wrap my head around this. Her doctor told her 40% to survive 2 months and then moved it to 15% to survive a month. They are encouraging her to enter hospice - though she has rejected that for now because she doesn't want our kids to see her NOT fight as much as she can. The treatment she needs is a bone marrow transplant, but due to her other health issues it's not possible. So she's on a million pills trying to hold on as long as possible. I'm sad, but trying to save it for when the bad happens. But I'm coping poorly. There's nobody in my life I have to really talk to, so there is my little bit of screaming into the void. Fuck.
item.dc:creator: ixyylt

item.title: Sean Astin being a gem
item.description: Not my tictac, not my depression. But if you're that person with the severe depression, please rest assured that it does get better, and that there are people who are absolutely and actively interested in making sure that it does get better. You are not alone.

item.title: Small moments of validation
item.description: People always assumed I was giving the cold shoulder when I got upset. I couldn't explain I couldn't speak. The words were there but I couldn't get them out. Especially if someone is in my face. I began to believe people, that I must be toxic. Decades later I'm realizing I'm audhd. It was always there. Even had pediatric psychologists confirm it. But the narrative I was fed by my NPD mom was they didn't know what the ADHD drugs would do and I was just a little too social. Yes. The child who enjoys playing by herself and didn't have friends until the second grade is too social. Anyway, so, finally getting to a place where I'm embracing these qualities and finding much more effective methods to address them. Recently started using note pads to communicate. Today I was tired, over stimulated, and started having a cold. Cherry on top I had to make decisions. First of my toddler actually needs new shoes or if he's pretending that everything hurts. Then grocery shopping. Last ordering fast food. Do I want a chili from Wendy's or a Cobb salad. Immediately freeze. I can't. I just can't. And I was talking about something that's been stressing me at work. Suddenly no words. Except "I'm going nonverbal." And I didn't get shame, or criticism, or judgment. I took a second and said salad. And we drove the five minute trip home in silence as I began to regulate. And I was able to start speaking again. And not have a meltdown. Idk how I'm so lucky to have a partner that just holds space. I'm definitely not a healthy person. A lot of trauma and untreated or undiagnosed disorders. I'm working every single second of the day. But, it's taken a long time to get to this point. He's suffered so much. Every time I ask why he's still here it's something like despite everything I've put him through I still make more of an effort than anyone else in his life. The bonus of an audhd spouse is I have multiple hyperfixations so inevitably I'll have some important information. Or I have really strong pattern recognition. Saved his life several times catching ailments that if left untreated would have caused a disability or even death. Have just jumped into action when he couldn't. Idk. The thankful season is here, I'm thankful for my husband.
item.dc:creator: Wrongclusterbdisorderbutgooff

item.title: Missing in Denver
item.description: EDIT: ALLIE HAS BEEN FOUND AND IS SAFELY BACK AT HOME. THANK YOU <3 I never post this kinda thing here. If anyone is in the Denver area please keep an eye out. She was sleeping over a friends last night and went on a walk to make a phone call. She was last seen on stout and 26th 3 minutes from Cervantes. I talked to her last at 9 pm on the phone and she said she was on her way back. Her phone has been dead since. She was wearing a black fur coat, black pants and docs. Red hair in a high bun with a headband.

item.title: The Big Chonk Restoration: Update #2!
item.description:
Hey again, all! I'm a week into this project and I've got some good news to share! If you missed the first post of the restore, it's over here: https://imgur.com/gallery/HfTbIhu A quick summary of where things left off last weekend: lots of screws missing, some parts missing, the entire register is filthy from top to bottom, the internals scream for death and aggressively resist turning over, and my back hurts from having to lift this stupid thing. :D Rather than get ahead of myself and go straight into shining up the chassis and making all the pretty-able things look pretty, I'm knuckling in and attacking the mechanical bits first. The observant among you will notice that the mechanism is on blocks rather than sitting on the edge of the desk, something I haven't actually tried before but was suggested by several of you guys. :)
The reason for the blocks are these two pieces on the underside. They interact with the drawer box and would be very unwise to remove. So, I fished an old landscaping beam I meant to throw out years ago and sawed it to length. Works pretty good!
Now, before I jump into the greasy gobbits of repair, I wanted to share more about this handy dandy reference book I was recommended by another collector. I bought it a while ago and it's been helping me better understand the different features of each model, the history behind them, as well as the proper names for them. Below are a few entries I found helpful at the outset of this restoration because they deal specifically with the same model of register as the one I'm working on.
This is a motorized version of the machine I'm tinkering with, but it's from the same Class 400. The main difference being that the encasement to the far right contains the DC motor that operates the internal mechanisms in lieu of a crank. Other than that, all the rest of the features are identical to what I've got on mine.
Another slightly different version of the Class 400 with a metal cash box rather than wood. Fun fact, the wood box on mine isn't mahogany like I was lead to believe! It's actually oak. Leave it to collectors to embellish the value of their collection ;) I included this because of the accessory sitting on the printer box. NCR just calls them numbering levers, and they were intended as a way to record extra information on the margin of the detail slip. Whether the proprietor chose to record a weight, a reference number, or a credit account number was up to them. Either way, it's pretty nifty!
Some additional information on the number printer. Since I'm unlikely to find the correct paper and ink for the printer, or clean the residue stuck inside of it enough to print a receipt, it's nice to be able to see what they're meant to look like. More on that later.
Couldn't help but add this one. It's the same model that I restored a couple of months ago! https://i.imgur.com/mZjUaGv.jpeg
*Extremely* helpful was this diagram. Being able to have the names of each part of the crank mechanism helped me make sure the problems this machine is having weren't due to something being broken or missing. Digging around the internals of these registers is still one of my weaker relevant skills, so having something to look back to is like having a safety net in case I goof something up.
A side view of the printer, although this one is a lot cleaner than the one I inherited. @_@
Speaking of which, back to the actual register. The problem I'm dealing with right now is that this register sat somewhere for a very long time, collecting moisture and dirt until everything just seized right up. Part of the reason for that hard operation is this printer. For the register to work, the printer has to work. The crank operates both simultaneously through a shared shaft. Since the printer is on the very end of that shaft (nice) it has the most opportunity to cause the strongest resistance. Which is what was happening. This photo is what the printer looks like *after* I finished wiping off as much grease, rust, and grime as I could get at. If I wanted to take it apart and get into the internals - something I'm not nearly confident enough to attempt - I'd need to remove that cam wheel at the top. I did attempt to do just that, however the pin holding it in place is so utterly stuck in place that I gave up beating on it and will nobly leave that to the next person. I was able to hit it with enough penetrating oil to finally free up what was stuck, however the issue now is that there's still plenty of grime trapped inside the printer itself that I can't access. If I did feed paper into it, I'm pretty sure I'd very quickly make that problem much worse.
Here I knocked off some of the old grease and grime trapped between the printer and the main mechanism. It took some doing, but it's moving freely now. My hope is that with more use, it'll continue to loosen up.
Something I discovered while working is this custom stamp pad built into one of the printer's rollers. I had to soak it in isopropyl alcohol and work it around with a brush to loosen the dried up, cruddy ink blobs. It's difficult to read, even artificially flipped the right way around, but here's what it says: Citizens Milling and Elevator Co. Flour, Feed, Cement, and Corn Elevator and Cold Storage Feed Grinding Telephone: 118 -- Thorp, Wi. Other than a line entry on a historical website, I wasn't able to find any information about this business beyond what the receipt would have contained. I'm pretty sure if I want to know more, I would need to make a drive out to Thorp and start asking around to see if anyone remembers this company or knows who owned it. Still, it's more than I usually discover about these registers!
Lacking proper printer ink, I snapped a ballpoint pen in half and goobered up the roller. This is the best I could squeak out of it before giving up. For the record, this is the wrong size receipt paper. I just smooshed it against the pad manually.
Anyway! As for the rest of the register, this was the bastard mechanism that was causing so much unnecessary grinding and gear skipping in the last post. It's hard to tell, but this is one of six identical mechanisms behind each column of buttons. It turns out not only was this so gummed up with crud that none of it refused to work, but getting it to start moving again involved me hosing it down with penetrating oil and manually pushing and pulling that stud at the very center until enough of it worked between the metal. That post the stud is attached to moves maybe three, four millimeters either way. I spent all of Monday and Tuesday evening sitting at the workbench spraying, forcing this thing forward and back, and absolutely ruining my fingertips in the process. These things were bastards to loosen up.
But, eventually I was able to get each of them flushed, relubricated, and working properly! If you watch closely, you can see just how many actions are involved with this single bank of buttons. If just one part stops working, it all stops working.
While I was whipping out the lube, I took a few minutes to spray down the counters with alcohol and wipe them clean. It's the little stuff. You can also see how absolutely filthy the button caps are. Some of these I'll be able to clean up. A few, like that K key, are going to need to be replaced outright. I asked the guy I usually go through if he could make a replacement set for the register and he decided to try to hose me to the tune of $75, plus insisting I'd need to cut each key ring off and buy new ones from him for $3 a pop. Given this register has 60 keys... nah, fuck that. For that much money, I'd just as soon buy another register to work on. So the plan will be to track down replacements for the worst keys and leave the rest in place, wear and tear included. Honestly, I'm just aiming to replace that K key.
I'm several days in on cleaning, lubricating, and cranking this thing so it can loosen up a little more each time. My bench is a contained mess, I'm burning through shop towels and gloves like a madwoman, but little by little this thing is starting to come alive again.
It's a little squeaky, so I'll keep dripping oil into the relevant bits until it quiets down, but it's a sight better than where it was! Had I known the condition the internals were in before I bought it, I think I would have haggled a little harder. They're still not great, but they're coming along. Hopefully by next weekend (assuming family doesn't abduct me for Thanksgiving) I'll have a little more progress to show for all the bits of flesh I've knocked off my knuckles. :D
Until then, it's back to shitposting horses! Thanks for everyone's interest and encouragement! (Horse Source: Baron Engel)
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse
